coredata.dc:title: Remediation of groundwater contaminated with dye using carbon dots technology: Ecotoxicological and microbial community responses
coredata.dc:creator: Beker, Sabrina A.
Khudur, Leadin S.
Krohn, Christian
Cole, Ivan
Ball, Andrew S.
coredata.dc:description: Groundwater pollution poses a serious threat to the main source of clean water globally. Nanoparticles have the potential for remediation of polluted aquifers; however, environmental safety concerns associated with in situ deployments of such technology include potential detrimental effects on microorganisms in terms of toxicity and functional disruptions. In this work, we evaluated a new and ecofriendly approach using carbon dots (CDs) as Fenton-like catalysts to catalyse the degradation of dye-containing groundwater samples. This investigation aimed at evaluating the efficacy of a novel remediation technology in terms of dye degradation and toxicity reduction while assessing its impacts on aquatic microorganisms. Uncontaminated Australian groundwater samples were spiked with methylene blue and incubated in the dark, at 18 °C, under slow agitation, using CDs at 0.5 mg mL−1 and H2O2 at 73.5 mM for 25 h. The dye degradation rate was determined as well as the toxicity of the treated solutions using the Microtox® bioassay. Further, to determine the changes in the groundwater microbial community, 16 S rRNA sequencing was used and evenness and diversity indices were analysed using Pielou's evenness and Simpson index, respectively. This study revealed that dye-containing groundwater were effectively treated by CDs showing a degradation rate of 78–82% and a significant 4-fold reduction in the toxicity. Characterisation of the groundwater microbiota revealed a predominance of at least 60% Proteobacteria phylum in all samples where diversity and evenness were maintained throughout the remediation process. The results showed that CDs could be an efficient approach to treat polluted groundwater and potentially have minimum impact on the environmental microbiome.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Methylene blue dye
Groundwater remediation
Carbon dots
Toxicity
Microbial community characterisation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Groundwater sampling, site location and initial chemistry characterisation
Experimental design, catalytic degradation of MB and chemical parameters
Bioluminescence inhibition testing of GW samples (microtox assay)
Catalytic degradation of MB in groundwater by CDs/H2O2
Characterising the bacterial and archaeal community of groundwater

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The location of the groundwater sampling sites at Ballan (Victoria, Australia). Images from Google maps 2021.

Degradation of MB in groundwater over 25 h incubation period. A) Degradation of MB in groundwater GW37 samples. B) Degradation of MB in groundwater GW38 samples. Conditions: [MB]: 10 mg L−1; [CDs]: 0.5 mg mL−1; [H2O2]: 73.5 mM; Temperature: 18 °C; pH for GW37: 6.98; pH for GW38: 7.42.

Microtox assay data showing the 5-, 10- and 15-min inhibitory level at T0 and T25 (T0 initial and T25 final time). A) Luminescence inhibition of A. fischeri to groundwater samples GW37. B) Luminescence inhibition of A. fischeri to groundwater samples GW38. Asterisks indicate a significant difference.
Pielou's index (evenness) and Simpson index (diversity) for GW37 and GW38 (N = 10, n = 1).
Distribution and relative abundances of prokaryotes by groundwater sample at phylum level (N = 10, n = 1).

Non-metric multi-dimensional Scaling (NMDS) plot depicting the sample differences between the microbial community composition of the groundwater wells (N = 10, n = 5). Data ellipse shows the t-distribution at a level of 0.95.

Differently abundant taxa in GW38 compared to GW37 (N = 10, n = 5) identified with ANCOM-BC at a Holm's-corrected p-value of 0.05. Abundances were merged to genus-level prior to analysis. Unknown or unclassified ASV taxonomy was merged to the next known taxonomic level.
Heatmap of relative abundance of microbes at class level (N = 10, n = 1). Grey cells indicate that the taxon was not present.

Turbidity (NTU), dissolved oxygen (mg L−1) values and colour (OD λ 664nm) for GW37 and GW38 at final time. Different superscript letters (a, b, c, and d) denote significant differences between treatments of the sample groundwater sample.

head.ce:title: Remediation of groundwater contaminated with dye using carbon dots technology: Ecotoxicological and microbial community responses

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, RMIT University, Bundoora, VIC, 3083, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, RMIT University, Bundoora, VIC, 3083, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Manufacturing and Fabrication, School of Engineering, RMIT University, Melbourne, VIC, 3000, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Manufacturing and Fabrication, School of Engineering, RMIT University, Melbourne, VIC, 3000, Australia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Science, RMIT University, Melbourne, Victoria, 3083, Australia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Groundwater pollution poses a serious threat to the main source of clean water globally. Nanoparticles have the potential for remediation of polluted aquifers; however, environmental safety concerns associated with in situ deployments of such technology include potential detrimental effects on microorganisms in terms of toxicity and functional disruptions. In this work, we evaluated a new and ecofriendly approach using carbon dots (CDs) as Fenton-like catalysts to catalyse the degradation of dye-containing groundwater samples. This investigation aimed at evaluating the efficacy of a novel remediation technology in terms of dye degradation and toxicity reduction while assessing its impacts on aquatic microorganisms. Uncontaminated Australian groundwater samples were spiked with methylene blue and incubated in the dark, at 18 °C, under slow agitation, using CDs at 0.5 mg mL−1 and H2O2 at 73.5 mM for 25 h. The dye degradation rate was determined as well as the toxicity of the treated solutions using the Microtox® bioassay. Further, to determine the changes in the groundwater microbial community, 16 S rRNA sequencing was used and evenness and diversity indices were analysed using Pielou's evenness and Simpson index, respectively. This study revealed that dye-containing groundwater were effectively treated by CDs showing a degradation rate of 78–82% and a significant 4-fold reduction in the toxicity. Characterisation of the groundwater microbiota revealed a predominance of at least 60% Proteobacteria phylum in all samples where diversity and evenness were maintained throughout the remediation process. The results showed that CDs could be an efficient approach to treat polluted groundwater and potentially have minimum impact on the environmental microbiome.

ce:section.ce:para: Groundwater is a crucial natural resource across the world that is continually threatened by anthropogenic pollution. Groundwater is widely used as the main source of drinking water in many countries, as well as for irrigation, agriculture and industrial use (Abiriga et al., 2021). A variety of contaminants can reach groundwater aquifers due to the percolation of industrial effluents, sanitary landfills, accidently spills, illegal dumps, agricultural chemicals etc., that leach from surface waters and eventually find their way into the groundwater (Belle et al., 2021; Menchen et al., 2017). Such pollution may last for decades or centuries, posing a serious threat to groundwater safety. Among industrial water pollution, the World Bank estimates that approximately 17–20% comes from textile dyeing and finishing treatment given to fabric (Chan et al., 2011). Water containing dyes can adversely affect both ecosystem and human health if inadequately treated (Qian et al., 2020). Several issues may arise from the improper disposal of water containing dyes which can have irreversible damage on water sources such as freshwater and groundwater (Yang et al., 2018).

As concerns about contamination increase, there is a growing need for technologies to remediate dye-contaminated groundwater. Dyes are resistant to biodegradation; therefore, bioremediation is not suitable to treat such toxic contaminants. However, in situ chemical oxidation appears to promote the aquifer remediation of several environmental pollutants especially using nanomaterials such as nanoparticles and nano-sized zero-valent iron (nZVI) technology. Several recent reports have confirmed that chlorinated solvents, pesticides, and dyes can be successfully treated by injecting stabilised ZVI nanoparticles into a contaminated source zone (Fu et al., 2014; Mueller et al., 2012; Qian et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2018). However, this type of nanomaterial exhibited high toxicity by decreasing the growth rates of freshwater and marine organisms (Zabetakis et al., 2015); moreover, the presence of ZVI nanoparticles shifted and negatively impacted aquatic microbial communities in previous studies (Wu et al., 2019; Kocur et al., 2016) which may limit their in situ application. However, recently, innovative nanotechnologies based on less toxic carbon nanomaterials have emerged as an alternative approach, overcoming the environmental limitations of current chemical oxidation remediation.

Over the last decade, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted attention due to their unique physicochemical properties that have been exploited in catalysis (Sun et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2013; Mehta et al., 2019). As an emergent class of carbon nanomaterials with low toxicity and rapid electron transfer features, CDs show significant potential for application in chemical oxidation reactions such as Fenton-like catalysis for environmental remediation (Dhenadhayalan et al., 2020). Due to the high number of oxygen-related groups on the surface of the CDs, they can act as electron donors which can be increased by the presence of H2O2, a strong oxidant that can readily capture electrons (Wang et al., 2013). Our recent investigations showed that CDs can be applied in the catalytic degradation of different dyes under varying pH values and temperatures and can minimise the toxicity of the resultant effluents (Beker et al., 2020, 2022). These investigations overcame a number of the main limitations of current Fenton-like catalysts (Beker et al., 2020). However, there remain important research gaps to be addressed such as the outcome of this technology using environmental samples as well as fundamental knowledge of microbial community structure and dynamics to predict their impact on eventual deployment and release in the environment.

Therefore, this study aimed to investigate the efficiency of CDs as Fenton-like catalysts for the degradation of a model pollutant dye, methylene blue (MB) in groundwater as a remediation strategy while addressing environmental toxicity and impact on the aquatic microbiota aspects. It is important to develop and investigate environmentally friendly remediation technologies that can effectively treat contaminants whilst preserving the ecosystem. Hence, this work assessed for the first time the catalytic activity of CDs in the remediation of MB in Australian groundwater at a laboratory scale. To assess the toxicity of the treated samples, the inhibition of Aliivibrio fischeri (A. fischeri) using a Microtox® assay was evaluated. The effects on the diversity of the bacterial community structure were also evaluated using 16 S rRNA high throughput sequencing. Moreover, water quality parameters such as dye degradation, turbidity, dissolved oxygen were monitored throughout the experiment to evaluate the remediation efficiency.

Methylene blue (C16H18ClN3S), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) 30%, citric acid (C₆H₈O₇), iron (III) citrate (C6H5FeO7), urea (CH₄N₂O) and activated charcoal (AC) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich®. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (18 MΩ).

Iron and nitrogen-containing carbon nanodots (CDs) were prepared using a hydrothermal method as reported previously (Beker et al., 2022). Briefly, citric acid (1.0 g), urea (0.5 g) and ferric citric (1.0 g) were used as precursors. The reagents were dissolved in ultrapure water (30 mL) and stirred until homogeneous. The solution was transferred to a poly (para-phenol)-lined (PPL) stainless-steel autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 230 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the brownish CD suspension was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and the supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane to remove larger particles. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried and kept at −20 °C for further use. Characterisation of the CDs was performed by photoluminescence (PL), ultraviolet visible (UV–vis), zeta potential measurements, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) as reported by Beker et al. (2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Groundwater sampling, site location and initial chemistry characterisation
ce:section.ce:para: Groundwater samples were collected from monitoring wells located in Ballan, VIC, Australia by Environmental Earth Sciences International (EESI) Group (Fig. 1 ). Samples from two different uncontaminated wells (GW37 and GW38) were sampled using a MicroPurge® MP10 Controller. Disposable bladders and push-in tubing were used for each sampling point while the reusable materials were decontaminated using Decon 90 between the samples. During sampling, 2 L of non-contaminated groundwater was collected from each well and immediately stored at 4 °C in the dark until further use. Samples were sent to an accredited analytical laboratory (Australian Laboratory Services (ALS) and chemical parameters were comprehensively analysed.

Most of the solutes in the groundwater samples were present at levels below the limit for drinking, irrigation, and ecosystem standards (Table S1). Some parameters such as TDS, chloride, nitrate, manganese etc. varied between samples; however, they were all within the Australian guideline's limits. The pH values were between 6.98 and 7.42 which are considered to be near the optimal pH for catalytic degradation of dyes (pH 7.0–8.0) (Beker et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental design, catalytic degradation of MB and chemical parameters
ce:section.ce:para: Microcosms containing 100 mL of groundwater were spiked with 10 ppm of MB to simulate dye contamination. Microcosms containing MB were spiked with CDs at 0.5 mg mL−1 and 73.5 mM of H2O2. Respective controls containing only CDs or H2O2 were routinely included. The microcosms were kept in the dark, under slow agitation (80 rpm), at 18 °C for approximately 25 h. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

The catalytic performance of the CDs for MB degradation in groundwater was examined using UV–visible spectrophotometry. The CDs were dispersed in the MB/GW solutions and shaken in the dark for 1 h to achieve adsorption equilibrium. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was added to start the degradation reaction, and samples were taken at various time intervals to measure the degree of degradation, recorded by measuring a decrease in the maximum absorbance of the solution (λ max 665 nm). The degradation ratio was defined as:
Degradation ratio (%) = [(Ai-At)/Ai] × 100;
where A
i is the initial absorbance of the solutions and A
t is the absorbance of the contaminated groundwater at any time interval.

A set of chemical parameters were monitored throughout the experiments to evaluate the quality of the water. Dissolved oxygen (DO) was assessed using a HQ40d Portable Meter Kit (HACH). Turbidity was measured using a 2100 P Portable Turbidimeter (HACH) following the manufacturer's instructions.

After the incubation period, some of the samples still contained residual H2O2; therefore, an extra step was performed which included using activated charcoal (2 mg mL−1) to remove the remaining H2O2 from the samples (Aguinaco et al., 2011). The concentration of H2O2 was monitored by measuring the samples absorbance at a wavelength of 240 nm using the extinction coefficient of 43.6 M−1cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioluminescence inhibition testing of GW samples (microtox assay)
ce:section.ce:para:
The toxicity of the GW samples before and after remediation was assessed by inhibition of Aliivibrio fischeri (A. fischeri) using a Microtox® Model 500 Analyser following the manufacturer's instructions. In this test, the marine bacteria A. fischeri (Microtox reagent - MODERN WATER Microtox®) and the reconstitution medium were supplied by Streamline Hydro Pty. Ltd. Solutions containing 2% and 22% of NaCl were used as diluent and osmotic solution, respectively. Following reconstitution of the freeze-dried A. fischeri, the marine bacteria in solution were exposed to the samples and inhibition of bioluminescence was measured at 5, 10 and 15 min. All toxicity tests for each sample were performed in triplicate. Luminescence inhibition (I %) was calculated based on the following equation where I
1 and I
0 are the luminosity of the sample and the blank solution, respectively (Ma et al., 2016):
I % =(1-I1/I0) × 100%

Total DNA of groundwater samples was extracted from the pellets of 100 mL of groundwater from each biological replicate using MoBio Power Soil DNA isolation kits (Mo Bio Laboratories, Inc, USA) for both initial time (T0) and final time (T 25 h). Cells were obtained by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The pellets were resuspended and approximately 300 μL was transferred to the PowerBead Tubes supplied with the isolation kit, for subsequent DNA lysis and extraction, following the manufacturer's instructions. The final DNA was eluted in sterile DNase & RNase-free water and stored at −20 °C until further analysis. The biological replicates of each treatment were pooled and sent to the Australian Genome Research Facility Ltd (AGRF) in Melbourne, VIC, Australia, for sequencing. DNA was sequenced on an Illumina MiSeq platform (San Diego, CA, USA) after amplifying the V3–V4 region of the 16 S rRNA gene, using primer pairs 341 F (CCTAYGGGRBGCASCAG) and 806 R (GGACTACNNGGGTATCTAAT). A total of 575,974 quality sequences at a median frequency of 56,868.50 sequences per sample and 1651 unique amplicon sequence variants (ASVs) were obtained.

The ASVs were subsequently processed with QIIME2 (v2019.7) (Bolyen et al., 2019). The demultiplexed raw reads were primer-trimmed and quality-filtered using the cutadapt plugin followed by denoising with DADA2 using default settings (Callahan et al., 2016). A primer-specific taxonomic classifier was created based on Greengenes 16 S rRNA gene database reference sequences (v13.8 at 99% similarity) and taxonomy was assigned to ASVs using “q2-feature-classifier classify‐sklearn” (Bokulich et al., 2018).

Alpha diversity indices were created on rarefied abundances in R-Studio (www.rstudio.com) with package phyloseq (‘estimate_richness') and a sample size equal to the minimum reads (44,666) for resampling (McMurdie and Holmes, 2013). Simpson index of Diversity (1-D) was calculated based on D = sum (pi 2) and is giving more weight to abundant ASVs. Pielou's Evenness (J′) equals Shannon entropy/log (Richness) and represents the distribution of ASVs within a sample. Ordinations of non-metric multidimensional scaling were created using ‘phyloseq:ordinate’ with Bray-Curtis dissimilarities after filtering raw abundances to a minimum of 10 reads per ASVs, which resulted in a total of 800 filtered ASVs. Permutational multivariate analysis of variance (PERMANOVA) was performed with help of the adonis package using the same dissimilarities and filtering. The package ANCOM-BC was used to differentiate abundances between wells GW37 and GW38, based on log-ratio transformations and the setting ‘group = NULL’ (Lin and Peddada, 2020). Differentially abundant taxa on genus-level were compared at a Holm's-corrected p-value of 0.05.

One-way analysis of variance for multiple data (ANOVA) was performed on groups of data followed by post-hoc t-tests between treatments to determine significant differences (at 0.05 significance level). Mean values are shown as the average data and standard deviation of three replicates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic degradation of MB in groundwater by CDs/H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic efficiency of CDs in the Fenton-like degradation of MB in Australian groundwater was tested for the first time. As demonstrated by our recent research, CDs can act as Fenton-like catalysts for the degradation of dyes in water under optimised conditions (Beker et al., 2020). The degradation rate of MB in both GW37 and GW38 showed that the highest degradation rates over 25 h were achieved by the treatment using CDs/H2O2 (Fig. 2 a and b). At the end of the incubation period, both samples, GW37 and GW38 showed a degradation rate of 82% and 78%, respectively; treatments using only dots showed approximately 34% degradation for both samples. Controls amended with only H2O2 showed the lowest degradation rates, 22% and 21%, respectively.

The mechanism involved in the MB degradation is based on the Fenton-like reaction catalysed by CDs. As explored and reported in previous studies, the catalytic activity of CDs is likely due to their surface functionalities which consists of their surface chemical groups and charges (Beker et al., 2020, 2022; Arul et al., 2017). A strong electrostatic affinity between the negatively charged catalyst and the cationic dye that has an estimated pKa of 3.8 may contribute positively to the catalytic reaction. In our previous study, the CDs showed a surface charge of −37.9 mV measured by zeta potential at pH 8.0 (Beker et al., 2022). In the presence of H2O2, multiple surface functional groups such as amines, carboxylic acids, carboxyls, hydroxyls and Fe (III) are likely to increase the electron density and mobility in the CDs which could potentially accelerate the electron transfer from the CDs to H2O2 during Fenton-like catalysis. As a result, it increases the degradation and/or oxidation rate of pollutants by the enhanced generation of free radicals (Beker et al., 2020). Hydrogen peroxide was added to the system at an initial concentration of 73.5 mM and its consumption was assessed at the end of the incubation period. After 25 h, the H2O2 concentration was reduced to 41.97 mM and 42.86 mM for CDs/H2O2 treatment for GW37 and GW38, respectively. This revealed that the presence of CDs was able to partially decompose H2O2 leading to a significant reduction of 43% and 41.7%, respectively. In a similar study, H2O2 was decomposed by Fe-CDs with a rate degradation constant of 0.7 × 10−1 min−1, which is comparable with most efficient iron oxide-based catalysts developed to date (Bourlinos et al., 2017).

To remove the remaining H2O2 of the samples, an extra step was performed consisting of filtering by activated charcoal. Following activated charcoal (2 mg mL−1) treatment, the control presented H2O2 at 17.2 mM and 19.28 mM for GW37 and GW38, respectively, while the CDs/H2O2 presented 3.83 mM and 4.83 mM. The higher degradation efficiency of the CDs/H2O2 compared to control H2O2 was supported by the above results since the catalysis performed by the CDs was able to decompose higher levels of H2O2 which led to significant higher MB degradation rates.

Table 1 shows turbidity and dissolved oxygen measurements at the end of the incubation period. Turbidity, which is generally caused by suspended chemical and biological particles, can have both water safety and aesthetic implications for drinking-water supplies; therefore, its measurement is fundamental to assess whether the water treatment is effective. At time 0, turbidity values were above 10 NTU for all samples. After 25 h, turbidity values significantly decreased for all samples, and the lowest values (1.50 and 1.72) were observed for GW37 and GW38 samples treated by CDs/H2O2, respectively. Dissolved oxygen (DO) is also part of a chemical quality check of healthy water and is usually used as an indicator of the extent of eutrophication of the water body. The DO results showed significantly that values were kept within 7.81 and 10.4 mg L−1 for all samples and the values were slightly but higher for the treatments containing H2O2. In terms of visual decolourisation, the images in Table 1 show results in accordance with the degradation rates. The highest decolourisation rates were observed for samples treated with CDs/H2O2 as shown by the images and absorbance measurements (OD λ 665nm) (Table 1).

In remediation studies, evaluating the toxicity of post-treatment effluents is fundamental to predicting their environmental impact. Nevertheless, very little has been reported on the final toxicity of effluents treated using carbon-based materials, especially CDs and whether they can significantly reduce the toxicity of such effluents under Fenton-like conditions. Studies that have applied chemical oxidation to remove dyes showed higher toxicity in treated effluents (Marson et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2020; Alderete et al., 2021) or the same toxicity levels as the non-treated effluents and/or their parent compounds (Parolin et al., 2013; Dojcinovic et al., 2011). The consequent breakdown of the dyes during the degradation might lead to the formation of toxic by-products (Alderete et al., 2021; Rizzo, 2011; Vieira et al., 2021) which implies the need for further treatment to minimise its toxicity and deliver safe, treated water back into the environment. Our previous study indicated that the ecotoxicity of MB effluents were significantly reduced using iron and nitrogen-containing CDs as Fenton-like catalysts under laboratory conditions (Beker et al., 2022). In this study, the toxicity of the treated groundwater samples was tested after 25 h using the Microtox® bioassay.

For GW37 and GW38 samples using only H2O2 (control), the inhibitory levels were 95.12% and 95.61% at 15 min, respectively. However, for the CDs/H2O2 samples, the inhibitory results were significantly reduced showing an approximate 4-fold reduction in toxicity reaching only 20% and 26.17%, respectively. This showed that the Fenton-like degradation of MB in groundwater samples using CDs as catalysts was the only effective remediation treatment for both decolourisation and reduction of effluents toxicity. Methylene blue showed negative effects in both groundwater samples by inhibiting A. fischeri luminescence to 55.45% and 58.00% for GW37 and GW38, respectively, which increased at end of the experiment to 71.03% and 85.29% (Fig. 3 a and b). The toxicity of the MB-contaminated groundwater samples containing CDs showed similar results to the samples containing only MB; at time 0 they showed 73.57% and 60.87% and at time 25 similar inhibitory values of 75.95% and 64.59% were observed, respectively. The addition of CDs itself did not significantly increase the toxicity of the groundwater samples which revealed a low (or non) toxicity of the dots. These findings confirm that in terms of reducing ecotoxicity, the CDs were beneficial for treating dye contaminated groundwater. This important result contrasts with those previously reported when nZVI were applied to contaminated groundwater; the authors reported a toxic effect on the bacteria possibly due to the release of the iron ion or redox shifts (Zabetakis et al., 2015). Moreover, it has been reported that nZVI toxicity towards microbes is highly modulated by environmental conditions (Lefevre et al., 2016).

One of the main questions of this work was to address how CDs technology may impact groundwater microbiology. Groundwater microorganisms act as indicators of the overall aquifer health and perform several important ecosystem functions (Morrissy et al., 2021). For instance, the naturally occurring bacteria in groundwater transform organic carbon to carbon dioxide (CO2), transform nitrogen, iron, and sulphur between oxidised and reduced forms, which consequently may affect many groundwater quality parameters such as pH, redox status, dissolved oxygen etc. Moreover, groundwater ecosystems provide biologically mediated transformation and degradation of contaminants (Patil et al., 2014; Adetutu et al., 2015). Thus, research on groundwater remediation should include the study of the potential loss of biodiversity and consequently ecosystem functions (Morrissy et al., 2021).

To study treatment effects on the alpha diversity, the Simpson's Diversity index and Pielou's evenness were analysed at T0 and 25 h after treatments (Fig. 4 ). Biodiversity analyses of the pooled biological replicates revealed that overall, alpha diversity was unaffected by the treatments in both groundwater samples. The Simpson index values observed for GW37 and GW38 at initial time was 0.91 and 0.95, respectively, which indicated a high microbial diversity among the dominant species. At the end of the treatment, the Simpson values for GW37 samples have not declined except for the sample containing only MB, which showed a slightly reduced index to 0.84, suggesting that some dominant species may have been affected by MB. For the GW38 samples, all showed Simpson indices above 0.91, which indicated that diversity was not reduced throughout the experiment. These results suggest the overall health of the treated samples by the presence of a diverse microbiota, likely maintaining ecosystem functions (Dojcinovic et al., 2011; Wagner et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2021). Conversely, Castaño et al. found a significant reduction in alpha-diversity of microbial communities and alteration of the microbial composition in arsenic-contaminated groundwater samples when using nZVI technology. Their work showed a reduction in evenness and diversity indices possibly due to the high toxicity of arsenic in addition to the potential toxicity of nZVI that impacted the groundwater microbiota (Castano et al., 2021).

It was also noted that Pielou's evenness in GW38 was initially greater compared to GW37, hinting at a broader distribution of carbon and energy utilisation in GW38. Furthermore, in both wells, Pielou's evenness changed only slightly after the treatments (MB, CDs and H2O2), again suggesting that they did not disturb the uniformity of the groundwater microbial communities (Fig. 4). Initial samples displayed Pielou indices of 0.64 and 0.77 for GW37 and GW38 samples, respectively. At the end of the treatment, evenness indices for GW37 and GW38 were 0.79 and 0.72 for CDs/H2O2, respectively. An increase in the microbial evenness was observed for GW37 samples, while GW38 showed a slight decrease.

Based on the two groundwater wells investigated, these results show that diversity remained relatively unaffected by the technology proposed in this work. However, they require validation through ongoing research. Other studies proposing alternative technologies, such as nZVI addition potentially inhibited microbial functions that were important for polychlorinated biphenyls (PCB) remediation strategies (Tilston et al., 2013). Moreover, another study involving the use of nZVI for the removal of heavy metals in contaminated aquifers via in situ bioprecipitation mechanism showed that important groups of microorganisms were inhibited by iron nanoparticles (Kumar et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterising the bacterial and archaeal community of groundwater
ce:section.ce:para: A key focus of this work was to investigate the impact of the CDs technology on the aquatic microbiota. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the microbial communities of Australian groundwater containing CDs. Indeed, the Australian groundwater microbial community is largely unexplored. This work reveals for the first time the bacterial and archaeal diversity of groundwater wells located in Ballan, Victoria, Australia. The compositions of the communities prior to the contamination (T0) with MB at the phylum level are shown in Fig. 5 . Generally, Proteobacteria, Actinobacteria, Chloroflexi, Gemmatimonadetes, Acidobacteria and Bacteroidetes were the main phyla.

In terms of relative abundance, the groundwater samples at time zero showed approximately 60% of Proteobacteria followed by 8.6% of Gemmatimonadetes for GW37 and 5.1% for GW38. The predominance of Proteobacteria in Australian groundwater was reported by other authors. Korbel et al. (2021) reported that the most abundant phyla in Australian groundwater were Nitrospirales, Nitrosopumilales, Proteobacteria, Acidobacter and Actinomycetales with the presence of Archaea as well. Patil et al. (2014) found a predominance of Proteobacteria, Spirochaetes, Firmicutes, Methanomicrobiaceae and Methanosarcinaceae in microbial communities in PCE-contaminated Australian groundwater. Proteobacteria was also found as a predominant phylum in Norwegian (Abiriga et al., 2021), Chinese (Chen et al., 2019), Russian (Braun et al., 2016) and Spanish (Castano et al., 2021) groundwater environments.

At the end of the degradation experiment (25 h), the MB-containing samples exhibited a varied bacterial composition without showing a specific pattern or shifting among samples containing CDs or H2O2 (Fig. 5). However, the predominance of such phyla indicated that important and abundant species, essential for fundamental biogeochemical functions in natural environment could cope with the stress caused by the exogenous agents used in this work.

Proteobacteria are ubiquitous and dominant within various freshwater environments and display a vast functional range comprising phototrophs, autotrophs and heterotrophs that play essential roles in breaking down organics and keeping a balanced and healthy ecosystem. Specific examples include diverse and active populations of Pseudomonas which are known for their denitrifying capabilities in almost all its species and Nitrosomonas which are involved in nitrification and related processes (Wagner et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2021). Furthermore, Bacteroidetes which are one of the most abundant phyla in the groundwater are known as saprophytic organisms and possess the function of polymeric organic degradation (Dojcinovic et al., 2011). For example, Flavobacterium is widely known to play an important role in the degradation of complex biopolymers in marine and freshwater environment. Actinobacteria is essential in breaking down dead organics so that molecules can be well reabsorbed by plants. The presence of such phyla implies that some microorganisms can survive in waters containing CDs/H2O2.

To analyse the differences in microbial communities between the two groundwater samples, Non-Metric Multi-Dimensional (NMDS) scaling was performed. The NMDS ordination illustrated that the microbial community composition was different between the two wells as it grouped the samples based on their Bray-Curtis dissimilarities into two non-overlapping clusters at a stress value of 0.064 (Fig. 6 ). Permutational multivariate analysis of variance (PERMANOVA) using Bray-Curtis dissimilarities validated compositional differences between the two wells (Df = 1, F = 2.75, R2 = 0.26, p = 0.01). These results were further corroborated by the microbial characterisation analysis, further discussed in the following section. The NMDS further suggested that microbial composition of the samples treated by CDs and H2O2 remained like the initial time as they remained grouped into GW37 and GW38.

To assess which specific genera were differently abundant between the two well communities, Analysis of Compositions of Microbiomes with Bias Correction (ANCOM-BC) was performed. This provided further insights into the niches of each well environment as it selected some specific bacteria. ASVs belonging to five taxa were almost exclusively present in GW38 samples and not found in GW37. These included three uncultured/unclassified bacteria in the classes GKS2-174 (Candidate phylum GN02/Gracilibacteria), ZB2 (Candidate phylum OD1/Parcubacteria) and TK17 (Chloroflexi). Furthermore, ASVs belonging to the family ACK-M1 (Actinobacteria) and genus Thermomonas spp. (Proteobacteria) were present predominantly in GW38 (Fig. 7 ). Conversely, ASVs belonging to 15 taxa were dominant in the GW37 well, including the Families Rhodospirillaceae and Koribacteraceae, as well as the genera Lutibacterium, Aquabacterium, Lysobacter, Nocardia and Desulfobulbus. These genera are widely found in water and soil, playing different and important roles; for example, Aquabacterium were isolated from drinking water distribution systems in water biofilms, and they are known for their ability to metabolise a broad range of organic acids (Kalmbach et al., 2000). Desulfobulbus are involved in the sulphur cycle in aquatic sediments (Pagani et al., 2011) while Lysobacter are strictly aerobic Gram-negative bacteria and ubiquitous inhabitants of soil and freshwater habitats (Xie et al., 2012). The differences observed between samples may be due to differences in pH, dissolved oxygen, chemical elements, and other parameters as shown in Table S1, which may have impacted the selection of microorganisms for each well.

For further analysis of the composition of communities in groundwater samples, all abundances were merged to class level. A diverse set of bacterial and archaeal classes were detected in all samples. Heatmap analysis revealed that the largest class based on abundances across all samples, prior and following MB-contamination and treatment with CDs/H2O2, were from Betaproteobacteria, Gammaproteobacteria, Alphaproteobacteria, Deltaproteobacteria and Actinobacteria (Fig. 8 ).

These classes are abundant and ubiquitous bacterial genera that can be found in drinking water, including mineral water and they have been identified in Australian groundwater in previous studies (Adetutu et al., 2015; Ginige et al., 2013). For example, Adetutu et al. (2015) found TCE contaminated wells was largely dominated by Gammaproteobacteria. Other groups such Alphaproteobacteria, Betaproteobacteria, Deltaproteobacteria, Clostridia, Epsilonproteobacteria and Chloroflexi were also detected.

Fig. 8 showed that there was an increase of Betaproteobacteria for samples containing only CDs for both GW37 and GW38 when compared to the initial time (T0). For GW37, heatmap analysis showed a decrease of Betaproteobacteria in samples containing MB and H2O2 while for GW38 there was an increase in Betaproteobacteria in samples containing CDs/H2O2. However, the Betaproteobacteria population diminished when using only H2O2 for both groundwater samples. Gammaproteobacteria and Alphaproteobacteria displayed similar behaviour, showing an increased population for all samples except for the one containing only H2O2. Similarly, Fajardo et al. (2012) reported an increase in Alphaproteobacteria in the presence of nZVI in sandy clay loam; in contrast, a decrease in Beta and Gammaproteobacteria abundance was reported which is the opposite found in our work. Interestingly, the relative abundance of Actinobacteria increased in samples containing CDs/H2O2 for GW37 but not for GW38.

Some reports suggest that alternative treatments are likely to have long-term impacts on aquifer indigenous microbial community structure. In aquifer sediments, Kirschling et al. (2010) reported significant shifts in eubacterial diversity that were still apparent 250 days after nZVI addition. Kumar et al. (2014) also reported a shift in the microbial community structure in aquifer sediments from a community dominated by Acidithiobacillus ferrooxidans and Sulfobacillus-related species to a community dominated by Clostridium and Sporotalea propionica-related species 130 days after nZVI addition (Lefevre et al., 2016). Although the mechanism reported in Kumar et al. (2014) is different from the Fenton-like mechanism used in this work, the application of iron-based nanoparticles led to significant changes in the microbial community while Fenton-like approach using CDs functionalised with iron showed minimum disturbance in the groundwater microbiome. From a functional perspective, the introduction of nZVI into the environment will likely disturb the equilibrium between the functional microbial groups carrying out important biogeochemical processes (Lefevre et al., 2016).

Collectively, our results illustrated scenarios relevant to the novel use of CDs in environmental remediation. Over the remediation period, the bacterial community showed an adapted community which can deal with stress (contaminants) and changing environmental conditions, keeping its diversity and evenness, and potentially preserving its functionality. These outcomes associated with the efficient degradation rates and low toxicity output revealed a potential and novel remediation technique that could overcome several drawbacks that limit current groundwater decontamination techniques.

This study highlighted the use of iron and nitrogen-containing CDs as a remediation approach for the degradation of MB in groundwater. The CDs were selected as Fenton-like catalysts for the degradation of MB in groundwater due to their rich surface chemistry, surface charge and dye electrostatic affinity as demonstrated in our previous studies where the CDs/H2O2 system increased the catalytic degradation of environmental pollutants in water under mild conditions. Herein we showed that this remediation approach also works for groundwater. Over the incubation period (25 h), the CDs/H2O2 system showed high catalytic activity, resulting in the degradation of 78–82% of MB in Australian groundwater samples. Further, the treatment could significantly reduce the toxicity of the effluents and showed minimal effect on the microbial groundwater community. Evaluation of the 16 S rRNA gene sequencing performed both at the start and end of the remediation confirmed no loss in key environmental microorganisms followed by the remediation process which is one of the challenging limitations presented by current remediation technologies. Parameters such as diversity and evenness showed high indices; therefore, the number of individuals within a species was constant throughout the groundwater community which was dominated overall by Proteobacteria. From a functional perspective, the introduction of CDs into the groundwater will not perturb the equilibrium of bacterial functional groups carrying out important biogeochemical processes. As far as we know, this is the first report on the remediation of Australian groundwater contaminated with dye using CDs technology focussing on the ecotoxicological and microbial community aspects. Our study provides numerous insights into the use of CDs as an eco-friendly option for the treatments of dye-contaminated water, which might be exploited for future environmental remediation applications. Future studies should investigate the recyclability of CDs and consider bringing greater emphasis on the effect of environmentally relevant factors on CDs toxicity to provide a better and more comprehensive understanding of the ecological impacts related to the use of CDs, especially on groundwater functionality.

Sabrina A. Beker: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – Original Draft, Visualization. Leadin S. Khudur: Methodology, Investigation. Christian Krohn: Software, Formal analysis, Writing – Reviewing and Editing. Ivan Cole: Conceptualization, Writing – Review & Editing, Supervision. Andrew S. Ball: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing- Reviewing and Editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the Brazilian National Council for Scientific and Technological Development (CNPq) for the PhD Scholarship granted to Sabrina A. Beker (process number #200697/2017–0/GDE).
